name: glom (Python Data Transformation and Access)

performance.notes: Pure Python; overhead minimal for most use cases

agent_readiness.notes: Nested data access and transformation with excellent error messages. from glom import glom, Spec, T, Val, Coalesce. glom(data, 'a.b.c') — nested attr/key access. glom(data, ('a', 'b')) — tuple for iteration. glom(data, ['items.name']) — list comprehension. glom(data, Coalesce('user.email', 'contact.email', default='')) — try multiple paths. glom(data, T.upper()) — method call via T. glom(data, {'name': 'user.name', 'age': 'user.age'}) — reshape into new dict. Val('constant') — embed literal. Assign: glom(data, Assign('user.verified', True)). Spec objects composable. Error messages show exact path of failure. Used for API response parsing and data reshaping.

gotchas: Learning curve for complex Spec composition
Performance overhead vs direct dict access for hot paths
T proxy magic can be surprising to debug
Less intuitive than simple dict access for simple cases
best_when: Parsing deeply nested API responses safely
When KeyError/AttributeError at unknown depth is a problem
Data pipelines where error path visibility is important
avoid_when: Simple flat data — regular dict access is cleaner
Performance-critical hot paths (overhead vs direct access)
When pydantic or dacite already handles the transformation
summary: glom provides safe nested data access and transformation with excellent error messages. glom(data, 'a.b.c'). glom(data, ['items.name']). Coalesce('email', 'contact', default=''). T.upper() for method calls. Dict reshaping: glom(data, {'name': 'user.name'}). Assign for mutation. BSD-3-Clause by Mahmoud Hashemi. Best for safely parsing nested API responses and data reshaping. Learning curve for complex specs. Overhead vs direct dict access.
